response: Yes, it is possible to use a different `create_app` function when running a Flask app as a server versus when running a CLI command, but it requires careful setup. You can also pass parameters to `create_app` when running as a CLI command. This can be achieved without setting an environment variable, but it may require some clever use of the Flask CLI and the app factory pattern. One approach is to use the `flask` command-line interface to pass arguments directly to your CLI command. For example, you can use `flask my_command x` to pass a parameter to your CLI command. This parameter can then be used to determine which `create_app` function to use or to modify the configuration of the app. However, this approach still requires you to provide the parameter explicitly. If you want to avoid this, you can use the Flask CLI to trigger a specific behavior based on the context in which the command is run. For instance, you can check whether the CLI command is being executed as part of the app's context or not. This can be done by checking the `app` object within the CLI command. If the app is already created, you can use that instance to modify its configuration or behavior. If the app is not yet created, you can create it with a different configuration based on the CLI command. This way, you can differentiate between server mode and CLI mode without needing to set an environment variable.
